courses.title: Smart Manufacturing & Digital Twin
courses.summary: Industry 4.0 without the brochure: data architectures, digital-twin levels, simulation, and predictive analytics — evaluated with an engineer's skepticism and a maintenance career's use cases.
taught_from: NPTEL: Industry 4.0 and Industrial Internet of Things-family courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: The technology cluster named and sorted: connectivity, data, analytics, autonomy. Maturity models used as maps, not scorecards. Separating what is deployed from what is marketed.
From sensor to historian to lake: the data pipeline with its quality gates. Context — asset hierarchies and event framing — as the expensive part. Why analytics projects die of data preparation.
Descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive twins distinguished by what they can answer. Asset versus process twins. An honest inventory of what each level costs to build and keep true.
Discrete-event simulation for lines and buffers; validation against measured cycle data. Experiments on the model instead of the plant. The packaging line simulated for a debottlenecking decision.

Feature extraction from vibration and process data; models from thresholds to learning methods in outline. Validation against labeled failures — the scarce commodity. Where analytics augments and where it hallucinates.
Cameras, lighting, and defect detection on moving product. Classical vision versus learned models. False-reject economics on a high-speed line.
Scan-to-print for obsolete spares; digital part inventories. Qualification questions for load-bearing prints. The maintenance storeroom's digital future, soberly assessed.
Expanded attack surface of connected equipment; segmentation and monitoring revisited at plant scale. Incident response for OT. Security as a precondition, not a feature.
Pilot-to-scale economics; the graveyard of proofs-of-concept explained. Benefits tracked against baselines the finance department accepts. Engineering economics applied to the smartest buzzwords.

Evaluate a vendor's condition-monitoring-plus-analytics proposal for a representative plant: architecture, claims, gaps, and a pilot design with success criteria. The skeptical adoption skill this decade rewards.

courses.title: Maintenance Planning, Turnarounds & CMMS
courses.summary: The maintenance planning engineer's complete toolkit: work management flow, job plans, scheduling, backlog and KPIs, spares, CMMS/SAP PM, turnarounds, and budgets — all eight Core-60 planning lessons in one course.
taught_from: SMRP CMRP framework (practitioner curriculum; framework, not a textbook)
NPTEL: operations and project management content (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Identify, plan, schedule, execute, close, analyze — the loop drawn as the maintenance department's operating system, with role boundaries (requester, planner, scheduler, supervisor) made explicit. Where the loop breaks in real plants: unplanned work injection, closure without data. The map every other planning lesson locates itself on.
Notification quality standards; screening and duplicate control. Priority set by criticality and consequence, not by who shouts. The gatekeeping function that protects the schedule.

The planned job package: scoped steps, labor and duration estimates, parts, tools, permits, and drawings — assembled before the job needs them. Estimating methods and the feedback loop from actuals. The condensed example: a pump seal-replacement package built line by line, the planner interview's favorite question.

Weekly scheduling against capacity: crew hours netted for leave and standing work, jobs committed by priority and readiness. Schedule compliance measured without gaming. The coordination meeting run properly — operations equipment release as the binding constraint.

Backlog in crew-weeks: healthy ranges, aging analysis, and ready-versus-total distinction. The planning KPI set — schedule compliance, planned-work ratio, PM compliance, wrench time — each defined with its known manipulations. Reading a maintenance department's health from five numbers.

Equipment bills of materials as the planner's index; criticality-driven stocking policies, min/max arithmetic from the inventory course, and the insurance-spare decision. Storeroom discipline: bin accuracy, preservation, kitting. The spares lesson EQUATE-scale plants staff whole teams for.

The CMMS data model: functional locations, equipment, task lists, work orders, notifications — SAP PM's names used since the Gulf runs on them. Master-data quality as the system's foundation; history codes that make analysis possible. What 'we have SAP but no data' means and how it happened.

The turnaround as a project: scope freeze, worklist challenge, long-lead procurement, and the CPM schedule at thousands of activities. Execution control: progress, extra work, and the discovery-work process. Why turnarounds overrun — scope, always scope — and the premium the Gulf pays planners who prevent it.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL: Project Management

lessons.scope: Zero-based versus historical budgeting; the budget built from PM programs, predicted corrective load, and turnaround accruals. Cost tracking by equipment and failure code — where the reliability engineer's Pareto comes from. Defending a budget with failure data instead of adjectives.
Daily battle rhythm: progress capture, lookaheads, and constraint clearing. Field coordination of contractors — the HEISCO-scale interface. Handback and startup sequencing with operations.
Scoping service contracts; unit-rate versus lump-sum trade-offs. Contractor performance measured like internal crews. The owner's-engineer skill of buying maintenance work well.

A four-week simulated plant: notifications arrive, you plan, schedule, and report KPIs; then a mini-turnaround is planned end to end. The full course exercised as one continuous role-play. Portfolio output: a complete planner's package.

courses.title: Pressure Equipment & Piping
courses.summary: The hardware that holds the pressure: vessel and piping construction logic, materials and joints revisited at code level, flanges and gaskets, relief devices, and inspection/repair thinking for static equipment.
taught_from: NPTEL: pressure vessel and piping-related courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Vessels, exchangers, boilers, piping, and tanks as regulated equipment classes. Design-code logic in outline: allowable stress and joint efficiency concepts. Why codes exist — written after explosions.
lessons.src: NPTEL pressure vessel content; SOM 201 foundations

lessons.scope: Thin-wall theory recalled, then thickness logic with corrosion allowance. Head types and their stress behavior. Nozzles and reinforcement in concept.
Pipe schedules, fittings, and specs; flexibility and thermal expansion loops from the thermal-stress lesson. Supports: shoes, guides, hangers, springs. Reading a piping isometric fluently.

Flange classes and facings; gasket selection and seating stress from the bolted-joint lesson. Controlled bolting: sequence, lubrication, and torque versus tension. Why flanges leak in January and how to stop it.
Gate, globe, check, ball, and butterfly internals; stem sealing and packing. Valve selection by service. Maintenance-relevant failure modes: passing, seizing, packing leaks.
Relief scenarios and device types: spring PSVs, pilot-operated, rupture discs. Set pressure, accumulation, and backpressure concepts. Installation sins: inlet losses and isolated relief paths.

lessons.t: Fabrication and welding at code level
lessons.scope: The WPS/PQR system recalled under vessel and piping rules; NDT extents and hydrotest logic. Traceability from mill certificate to weld map. The fabrication paper trail HEISCO-scale shops live by.

Thickness surveys, CML programs, and inspection intervals from the asset-integrity course specialized to vessels and piping. Internal inspection findings and their dispositions. The inspector–engineer interface in practice.
Temporary repairs — clamps, wraps — with expiry dates that must be honored; weld repairs and their qualification. Rerating logic in outline. Management of change for pressure equipment.

Classic vessel and piping failures dissected: brittle fracture, CUI ruptures, dead-leg corrosion, relief-path isolation. Each traced through the lessons that would have prevented it. Institutional memory as curriculum.

For one vessel and its piping circuit: equipment dossier with design data, damage mechanisms, CMLs, relief basis, and repair history template. The record-keeping backbone of integrity work, built once by hand.

courses.title: HSE for Industrial Operations
courses.summary: NEBOSH-aligned depth on top of Year 2's safety course: risk management systems, process safety, occupational health, environmental control, and the safety leadership expected of engineers in Gulf plants.
taught_from: NPTEL: Industrial Safety Engineering (nptel.ac.in); NEBOSH IGC syllabus alignment (framework, not a textbook)

lessons.scope: Policy, plan, do, check, act — the management-system loop applied to HSE. Leading versus lagging indicators. Why paperwork systems fail without field verification.
lessons.src: NEBOSH IGC alignment; NPTEL: Industrial Safety

lessons.scope: Suitable-and-sufficient assessment; task-based versus area-based approaches. ALARP reasoning with cost-benefit honesty. Assessment as design input, not filing.

Major-accident hazards distinguished from occupational ones; barriers and the Swiss-cheese picture used precisely. HAZOP participation basics for mechanical engineers. The EQUATE-scale context of process safety.
Noise, vibration, dust, and chemical exposure with measurement basics. Hearing conservation and hand-arm vibration for maintenance trades. Health surveillance as long-horizon duty of care.
Load assessment and workplace design; the maintenance task's awkward-posture reality. Mechanical aids and job design. Musculoskeletal injury as the trade's chronic epidemic.
Emissions, effluents, and waste streams of industrial plants; permits and monitoring in outline. Spill prevention and response. The environmental file an integrity engineer inherits.
Scenario planning, alarm and evacuation systems, and drills that test rather than perform. Fire systems interface with maintenance. Command structure basics for engineers on scene.

lessons.t: Contractor and permit interfaces at scale
lessons.scope: Multi-employer worksites and the coordination duty; permit systems under turnaround load. Induction and competence verification. Where fabrication-yard practice meets plant rules.
Culture measured by what happens when nobody watches; just-culture response to error. The engineer's daily leadership: walkdowns, questions, and stopping work. Influence without authority, practiced.
Build the HSE plan for a maintenance shutdown package: risk assessments, permits, isolations, emergency arrangements, and monitoring. The integration exercise that mirrors real pre-shutdown reviews.

courses.title: Reliability Engineering II
courses.summary: From analysis to institution: RCM implementation and root cause analysis in practice, bad-actor elimination, reliability KPIs, and the design of a plant reliability program that survives management changes.

lessons.scope: Running a real RCM study: team composition, facilitation discipline, and the information the analysis eats. Then root cause analysis as its operational partner — evidence preservation, causal-factor charting, and the 5-why's honest limits. The condensed example: an RCA on a repeat seal failure, from timeline to verified systemic cause.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Reliability Engineering; SMRP CMRP framework

lessons.scope: Pareto analysis of failure records to find the vital few; bad-actor teams with closure discipline. The reliability KPI set — MTBF by asset class, top-loss trending, PM effectiveness — built from CMMS data with its coding sins acknowledged. One bad-actor case tracked from data to eliminated failure mode.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL: Reliability Engineering

lessons.scope: The program architecture: criticality analysis feeding strategy development, PdM routes, planning integration, and a living KPI system — sequenced as a realistic multi-year roadmap with early wins funding later phases. Organizational design: where reliability engineers report and why it matters. The lesson that turns this curriculum into a job plan.
FRACAS logic: capture, code, analyze, act. Failure coding taxonomies that analysts can actually use. The data pipeline that makes Weibull analysis possible years later.

Reliability input to procurement specs; maintainability reviews of vendor drawings. Access, isolation, and monitoring provisions bought cheaply at design time. The capital-project seat reliability engineers must claim.
Failure distributions driving stocking levels; Monte Carlo thinking for insurance spares. Pooling and vendor-held options. The reliability–inventory interface quantified.

Operating-envelope discipline and startup/shutdown stress; precision-maintenance standards as failure prevention. Operator care integrated with PdM findings. Reliability as everyone's output, engineered.
MOC review of modifications for reliability impact; temporary equipment and its expiry. Documentation updates as part of the change, not afterthought. The quiet failures MOC exists to prevent.
Program costs against avoided losses; the credibility problem of counting non-events. Baseline construction and honest attribution. Selling year three of a program in year one's budget cycle.

Design the complete reliability program for a described plant: assessment, roadmap, organization, KPIs, and first-year plan with budget. Presented as to a plant manager. The portfolio piece for reliability-engineer interviews.

courses.title: Capstone — Integrated Plant Project
courses.summary: One plant, every course: a semester-long integrated project that takes a described factory from asset register to reliability program, production plan, and improvement portfolio — the curriculum proven as a single working toolkit.
taught_from: Course synthesis across the entire curriculum

lessons.t: The plant brief and asset register
lessons.scope: The project plant described: process, equipment, history, and constraints. Building the functional-location hierarchy and asset register. The dataset every later phase consumes.
Criticality analysis across the register; maintenance strategy assigned per class with RCM logic on the top tier. Deliverable: the strategy matrix with justifications.
Technology mapping, routes, and intervals for the critical equipment; baseline campaign planned. Deliverable: the condition-monitoring program document.
Job plans for the top ten repetitive jobs; weekly scheduling process and KPI definitions. Deliverable: the planner's handbook for this plant.
Damage mechanisms, CML plans, and relief-device registers for the static equipment. Deliverable: the integrity plan section.
Capacity, takt, and schedule for the product mix; OEE state model defined from available signals. Deliverable: production plan with loss-accounting design.
Three costed improvement cases — one reliability, one changeover, one energy — with NPV and payback. Deliverable: the investment one-pagers.
Risk assessments and permit requirements woven into the job plans and shutdown scope. Deliverable: the HSE annexes that make the plans executable.

All phases assembled into one coherent plant-engineering dossier with an executive summary written last and read first. Internal consistency audit across sections. The document that demonstrates the whole pathway.

The dossier defended against structured challenge: assumptions probed, trade-offs justified. Feedback folded into the final portfolio. The dress rehearsal for the interviews the career section prepares.
